functrl.title: FunCtrl — AI Learning Map Platform
functrl.tech: FastAPI · Vue.js · ChromaDB · PostgreSQL · Gemini Pro · Docker · Railway
functrl.nature: Capstone project | 1st place, InnoServe national competition, AI Application Tools track 🏆
functrl.intro: Public video platforms like YouTube hold rich educational content, but suffer from fragmented information and the difficulty of locating a specific knowledge point inside long videos. FunCtrl combines semantic vector search with LLMs into a smart learning platform offering high-precision video search and dynamic learning-path generation.

sections.heading: Live Demo — Video Learning Page

images.alt: FunCtrl video learning page: embedded YouTube player with AI-generated summary on the left, 'Best Moments' timestamp cards on the right that jump to the exact segment
images.caption: Learning page: AI-generated "Best Moments" timestamps and key-point summary — click a card to jump to the segment

sections.intro: For every video the system generates "Best Moments" timestamps and a key-point summary: the cards on the right are retrieved from subtitle vectors by the RAG pipeline and refined by the LLM. Clicking one jumps straight to that segment — solving the core pain of pinpointing knowledge inside long videos.

sections.heading: System Architecture

images.alt: FunCtrl architecture: Vue.js frontend on Vercel, FastAPI backend with RAG pipeline on Railway, PostgreSQL for structured data and ChromaDB for vectors, answers generated by Gemini 2.5 Flash
images.caption: FunCtrl architecture: Vercel (Vue.js) → Railway (FastAPI RAG pipeline) → PostgreSQL / ChromaDB → Gemini 2.5 Flash

sections.intro: Vue.js frontend deployed on Vercel; FastAPI backend with the RAG pipeline (embed query → vector retrieval → prompt assembly) on Railway. Dual-database design: PostgreSQL for structured data, ChromaDB for vectors (MiniLM-L6-v2 for short text / bge-m3 for long text), with Gemini 2.5 Flash generating the final answers.

subsections.title: 1. Architecture & Tech Selection
bullets: Architecture lead: designed the backend and AI service architecture, choosing FastAPI with ChromaDB for large-scale semantic retrieval. Built 30+ RESTful APIs covering JWT auth, personalized recommendation, and quiz generation.

Deployment & cost control: containerized frontend and backend with Docker and deployed to Railway; later led a seamless migration to an on-prem lab server to cut API and hosting costs, demonstrating cost awareness and infrastructure operations skills.

subsections.title: 2. Retrieval Optimization & Normalization
bullets: L2 distance with normalization: cosine similarity proved slow at scale, so I proposed normalizing vectors and retrieving by Euclidean (L2) distance — mathematically equivalent to cosine similarity but significantly faster, improving latency under high concurrency.
Query Expansion: used Gemini to semantically expand user queries with synonyms and missing terms, effectively raising retrieval recall.

subsections.title: 3. Rigorous Quality Evaluation
bullets: NDCG adoption: introduced NDCG (Normalized Discounted Cumulative Gain) as the team's north-star metric for ranking relevance.
Expert validation: built an expert-scoring process — three human experts independently rated query relevance on 10 target videos, averaging scores to reduce individual bias.
Model iteration: measured the gap between system scores and ideal (IDCG) scores to benchmark open-source embedding models, stabilizing retrieval accuracy above 88%.

sections.heading: Architecture Highlights
bullets: Three-tier vector RAG: layered embeddings for titles / tags / subtitles — search titles and tags first, then drill into subtitle-level matching, finally re-ranked by Gemini with precise timestamp localization.

Async self-expanding corpus: to solve the cold-start problem, Python asyncio background tasks trigger automatic video download, speech-to-text, and semantic summarization whenever query confidence falls below a threshold — the ChromaDB corpus grows itself.

Dynamic learning paths: LLM-generated hierarchical learning maps persisted to the database, integrated with auto-generated quizzes and a progress dashboard to close the "search → learn → assess" loop.

prepagent.title: PrepAgent — Telegram AI Study Assistant with RAG Knowledge Base
prepagent.tech: n8n Cloud · Supabase pgvector · Python 3.12 · Google Gemini-2.0-flash · Telegram API
prepagent.intro: Turns a popular NCU statistics lecture series on YouTube into an always-on, conversational Telegram study assistant: Whisper transcribed 29 lecture files, which are chunked, embedded, and served through a fully cloud-native stack — the bot stays online even when my laptop is off, with a modular design ready for new subjects.

subsections.title: 1. Direct REST API calls — dodging SDK version conflicts
bullets: The Google Generative AI SDK pins AI Studio keys to v1beta and frequently hit dependency conflicts, so I dropped the SDK and called the gemini-embedding-001 REST endpoint directly with requests — lighter and far more stable.
Endpoint: https://generativelanguage.googleapis.com/v1beta/models/gemini-embedding-001:embedContent

subsections.title: 2. Vector type selection: halfvec to beat the dimension cap
bullets: gemini-embedding-001 outputs 3072 dimensions, but pgvector's ivfflat index caps the standard vector type at 2000.
I chose pgvector's halfvec (16-bit float) type, which raises the index cap to 4000 dimensions (3072 < 4000 — index builds fine).
Bonus: 50% less storage and memory, with negligible accuracy loss for a statistics-tutoring workload.

subsections.title: 3. Transcript cleaning & sliding-window chunking
bullets: Whisper transcripts carry a line-number\ttext prefix; chunker.py strips them during ingestion.
Chunking uses a 20-line window with 5-line overlap, preserving context across chunk boundaries so retrieval never cuts a concept in half.

sections.heading: System Workflow (n8n)
sections.code:
Telegram Trigger (incoming message)
  └──> Switch router (/ask questions, /quiz practice, /summary recap)
        └──> AI Agent orchestrator
              ├── Model: gemini-2.0-flash
              ├── Memory: Window Buffer (last 5 turns)
              └── Tool: Supabase Vector Store
                    └── Postgres match_documents function (top_k=5)
  └──> Telegram Send (reply)

sections.heading: Retrieval Quality Evaluation (Hit Rate@5)
sections.intro: Built an evaluation set of 16 statistics questions spanning the basic and advanced courses, with per-question ground-truth lectures established via transcript keyword scanning. The evaluation follows the exact same retrieval path as the live bot (RETRIEVAL_QUERY embedding → match_documents); a question counts as a hit if any top-5 chunk comes from the ground-truth lecture.
bullets: Hit Rate@5 = 75% (12/16); among the 12 hits, 11 ranked the correct lecture #1.

Miss analysis: for the 4 misses (ANOVA, standard deviation/variance, central tendency, discrete/continuous random variables), isolated testing showed the best similarity within the correct lecture was only 0.61–0.65, below the 0.65–0.76 of the wrong lectures — a genuine semantic gap, not a near-miss in ranking.

Root causes: (1) basic-course transcripts are highly colloquial and example-driven, semantically distant from textbook-style definitional queries; (2) ANOVA queries get absorbed by ANOVA-table content in the regression lectures (a reasonable confusion).
Improvement directions: hybrid search (keyword + vector), contextual chunking (prefixing chunks with lecture topics), and query rewriting.

sections.heading: Live Demo & Conversational Memory
sections.intro: The bot has conversational memory: after the user asks "what does Lecture 2 cover?", a bare /quiz automatically generates questions scoped to Lecture 2 — no need to re-specify.

images.alt: PrepAgent Telegram bot answering 'what does Lecture 2 cover', auto-summarizing the four core topics of descriptive statistics
images.caption: /ask — lecture-scoped key-point summaries

images.alt: PrepAgent Telegram bot receiving /quiz and, using conversation context, generating three practice questions with explanations for Lecture 2
images.caption: /quiz — follows conversation context to quiz on Lecture 2

webdesign.title: proNCU — Course Management System (Build × Security Refactor)
webdesign.tech: Laravel · Blade · SQLite · Eloquent ORM · Bcrypt
webdesign.nature: Sophomore web-programming course project (team lead) | Self-initiated security refactor, 2026
webdesign.intro: A course-management and scheduling system (enrollment, credit dashboard, timetable, calendar) I built as team lead in my sophomore year. Reviewing the old code before graduation, I found major room for improvement in security, authorization, and code quality — so I launched a self-initiated refactor, documenting the full diagnose → fix → verify cycle as evidence of engineering growth.

sections.heading: Diagnosis: 5 Flaws in the Old Code
bullets: Plaintext passwords: stored and compared in plaintext — one database leak exposes every user's password.
Unguarded routes: dashboard pages (home, timetable, calendar) had no auth middleware; typing a URL bypassed login entirely.
Schema defect: the password column was VARCHAR(20), while Bcrypt hashes are a fixed 60 characters — writes would truncate or fail.
No backend validation: login and registration accepted empty input straight into the database.
Mixed SQL styles: controllers mixed raw SQL (DB::select) with Eloquent ORM, hurting maintainability.

sections.heading: Refactor Details
bullets: Password hashing: registration now stores Hash::make() (Bcrypt) hashes; login fetches the user via Eloquent and verifies with Hash::check(); the migration was updated to lift the column-length cap.
Route protection: registered a custom sess middleware alias in bootstrap/app.php and wrapped all authenticated routes in Route::middleware(['sess'])->group() — guests are redirected to login.
Backend validation: added $request->validate() (required fields, minimum length) with matching frontend error prompts.
Full Eloquent migration: rewrote all raw SQL in the course and calendar controllers as Course::where(...) / Event::where(...) chains — far leaner code that follows Laravel MVC best practice.

sections.heading: Integration Testing
bullets: Auth interception: direct access to /home or /course is caught by the middleware and redirected to login.
Validation & hashing: empty submissions trigger the right errors; new accounts verified as Bcrypt hashes ($2y$12$...) in SQLite; wrong passwords are rejected.
Regression: enrollment, credit dashboard, timetable, and calendar CRUD plus all Ajax interactions work unchanged — the refactor broke nothing.

sections.heading: Takeaway
sections.intro: This refactor turned mistakes I had seen into mistakes I have fixed: practical password hashing, web boundary defense with middleware, input validation, and ORM abstraction — a complete security-hardening cycle. Revisiting my own sophomore code was the most direct measure of how far I've come.

aicup.title: Smart Racket Player Classification & Attribute Recognition
aicup.tech: Python · DSP (FFT / Wavelet) · XGBoost · Random Forest · Feature Engineering
aicup.nature: AI CUP 2025 national competition | Rank 41 of 633 teams (top 6.5%)
aicup.intro: The task: build a multi-task classifier that identifies a player's skill level and swing attributes from accelerometer and gyroscope time-series captured by a smart table-tennis racket. Off-the-shelf RNN/LSTM/GRU models stalled at 0.3–0.5 accuracy because time-domain features drowned in sensor noise.

sections.heading: The Breakthrough: Digital Signal Processing
bullets: Time domain: kept the fundamentals — peaks, valleys, standard deviation, and rhythm statistics of the raw swing signal.
Frequency domain: applied FFT to decompose signals into frequency components, distinguishing slow defensive strokes from high-frequency smashes.

Time-frequency domain: introduced the wavelet transform — swings are transient events, and wavelets preserve both the frequency content and the exact moment it occurs, while also acting as an excellent noise filter.

sections.heading: Implementation & Model Tuning
bullets: Feature engineering: combined FFT energy distributions with wavelet-coefficient statistics, using LLM-assisted scripting to rapidly build extraction pipelines and prune features by correlation.
Model pivot: abandoned overfitting-prone deep models for XGBoost and Random Forest, which are far more robust on high-dimensional tabular data.
Hyperparameter tuning: tuned tree depth, learning rate, and regularization with cross-validation to guard against overfitting.
Output design: Softmax heads for multi-class targets (skill level), Sigmoid heads for binary ones (handedness, fore/backhand).

sections.heading: Key Takeaway
sections.intro: This project taught me that for time-series data, high-quality feature engineering — especially DSP techniques — beats stacking deeper networks. It also proved my resilience: when accuracy plateaued, I dug into academic resources, researched independently, and broke through.

kaggle.title: Kaggle — China Real Estate Demand Prediction
kaggle.tech: Python · Pandas · LightGBM · XGBoost · CatBoost · SciPy · SHAP
kaggle.nature: Kaggle forecasting competition | 2nd place of 777 teams (team "Monopoly") 🏆
kaggle.intro: Forecast 12 months of new-home transaction value across 96 districts from only 67 monthly records per district. The core challenges — extreme data sparsity, high dimensionality, and no exogenous variables for the forecast horizon — were solved with deep EDA and robust feature engineering.

sections.heading: Deep Exploratory Data Analysis
bullets: Data profiling: time-series plots, heatmaps, and histograms to understand distributions and trends.
Dual correlation testing: ran both Pearson (linear) and Spearman (rank) correlations to counter sparsity, filtering outlier-driven artifacts and stabilizing feature selection.

sections.heading: Feature Engineering & District Clustering
bullets: Reusable feature logic: led the feature-list design — lags, EWMA dynamics, seasonality factors, and spatial features like neighboring-district differentials and structural ratios.
Dimensionality control: K-Means-based imputation and PCA compression distilled 1,000+ candidates into 106 key features.
District clustering: clustered the 96 districts by volume and trend, giving the team differentiated modeling bases for different market regimes.

sections.heading: Model Comparison, Backtesting & Explainability
bullets: Benchmark platform: compared classical ARIMA (stationarity tests, p/d/q tuning) against ML models on equal footing.
Expanding-window backtesting: monthly validation with time-series cross-validation, ensuring no future information leaks into the past.
SHAP explainability: verified that all 106 features contribute in ways consistent with real-estate economics.

sections.heading: Final Results & Risk Control
sections.intro:
Ensembled LightGBM, XGBoost, and CatBoost with blend weights solved by SciPy optimization, plus inference-time risk balancing (Zero-sector Override) to avoid extreme errors — finishing 2nd of 777 teams.

Proof of award and team identity: see the Credentials section — click the Kaggle certificate cards to view the official announcement and verification.
